item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels very Junji Ito. Well done!

item.title: Re: Orphaned Footnote?
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see above (Quatral on 21 Oct 2014) that it was probably obsoleted, as the text currently reads according to his suggestion. I'm going to yank the footnote. Nevermind…

item.title: Orphaned Footnote?
item.content:encoded: I can't figure out what footnote 3 is supposed to refer to. Nevermind, ignore me, nothing to see here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this takes a bunch of elements that, on their own, aren't that new (for example, the "impossible to block sight" had been explored in SCP-1350 and SCP-639 is all about screwing with visuals) and ultimately builds a fantastic, unnerving article with them.

item.title: Belated "Happy +100!"
item.content:encoded: Egads. I just remembered this one the other day, and couldn't sleep because of it. This is very deserving of its score. Very good short-form, creepypasta-throwback skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks alright to me, j0si. Thanks for noting the edit you made in the comments and the edit window!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first contribution to the site of any sort was to fix a misspelling on this article. Hope that's okay. Thanks for writing such a fantastic little horror piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. Not too original, but creepy and a bit sad. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Footnote #3 doesn't feel necessary, and it might work a little better if the interviewer just referred to M██████ as "SCP-2066-1-6" instead. it would be likely that they've already discovered SCP-2066's effect upon M██████, hence the reason for doing the interview, so it'd be likely they've already classified them as "SCP-2066-1-6" at that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, usually skips where you look at it and then it kills/transforms you in some horrific way bore the hell out of me. This one doesn't. The way it's presented in the interviews both creeps me out and breaks my heart.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That actually makes a lot of sense!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well done! I loved the use of the interviews, it was a diversion from the usual style but it worked really well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I was thinking of it, she still has some sense of proprioception (being able to tell where your body parts are). So she can't feel the walls, but she can tell when something's in her way.

…And yeah, in my headcanon she's a woman, but there's no way to tell since I censored her name and referred to her as "subject". So I guess her gender is open to interpretation, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, top billing!
Second, great article. The fear really lies in the interview logs, when you get to see how the skip is constantly "watching" these people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been a long time since I saw a good creepy humanoid. I have to say I was expecting what happened in the final addendum, but the buildup makes this a worthwhile read. I do have to ask how the guy was able to find the wall to punch it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've said it before, and I'll say it again. I'm not scared by this, but I'm creeped out to hell and back. Everything about this scenario/scip makes me shudder with the thought of it. Bleergh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually my second, my first was SCP-2052. I was definitely going for a much different feel with this one, glad to hear people are liking it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. I'm seconding FortuneFavorsBold here; the actual object is pretty creepy, although not mindblowing, but the superb interview logs push this into "great" territory. Not many people can pull off the vintage S1-type article successfully, and the fact that this is your first is just more impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The structuring is bold. You're going against a lot of the established norms of humanoid SCP characterization, by ignoring most of the standard format, and using the description to set up the interview instead of really describing the object. A risky move, but it pays off. This is very interesting. My only critique would be to give the interviewers a bit more personality, but that's a minor quibble here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't a bad one - I loved the interviews.

I almost wish it was a defense mechanism because the picture of a researcher going through his daily life while trying to ignore an unsettling headless humanoid (but, harmless) standing a variable distance from them amuses me to no end.

Though, it's good as it is. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this motion. All in favor say "Aye."

Seriously, though, it's good to see occasional throwbacks to the community's creepypasta days, especially if it's well-written and legitimately creepy, and this is both of those things. Upboats for days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GOOD.

This is a good skip, and I'm gonna tell you why.

It's the human element. This skip is excellent at describing the anomaly through interview. It's a risky choice, not a lot of people can pull it off anymore, but this skip does an excellent job at building suspense with a proper payoff at the end. And it does so by good characterization of the anomaly and the situation. It's the subtle things, like the building urgency and mania in -6's voice. The increased coldness and distance of the researchers over time. 2066 get—

Well… I should probably not spoil it.

SOMEONE ELSE READ THIS THING AND UPVOTE IT. I haven't read good unsettling horror for some time, it deserves recognition.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh. Ahh. Creepy. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Jarbatalapus, Mister Evans, Tagliafierro, and Aurra for helping on the forums and chat.
